worth_it_explanation: Pacing decides whether a line item spreads its inventory across the flight or burns it early, and under-delivery on a guaranteed deal turns into make-goods and a damaged client relationship, so both carry real revenue and trust risk. The check is quick. The delivery setting and the delivery indicator are both visible on each line item in Delivery, and the auditor compares them against what the deal is meant to do. The fix is usually a settings or targeting change rather than a rebuild, and it changes how the line item spends inventory from the next request forward.
intro: In Google Ad Manager (GAM), a line item's delivery setting decides how its booked impressions are spread across the flight, and the delivery indicator shows whether the line item is on track to meet its goal. A pacing setting that does not match the deal can exhaust inventory before the flight ends or leave a scramble at the close, and a guaranteed line item that falls short of its goal forces make-goods or refunds. These checks confirm that each line item's pacing matches its intent and that guaranteed line items are projected to deliver in full.

audit_checks.title: Line item pacing is front-loaded or uneven against the flight
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The delivery setting decides whether inventory is spread across the flight or spent early, so a setting that does not match the deal exhausts impressions before the end date or leaves a shortfall to chase at the close. The line item still shows delivery in reporting, so the uneven curve is easy to miss until the flight runs short.

educate.base:
In Google Ad Manager (GAM), the 'Deliver impressions' setting on a line item decides how its booked impressions are paced across the flight. There are three choices. 'Evenly' spreads delivery across the start and end dates so the line item stays close to on schedule for the whole flight. 'Frontloaded' serves ahead of schedule early, beginning as much as 40% above the daily goal and easing back toward the end. 'As fast as possible' ignores the schedule entirely and takes every impression it can without compromising line items at the same or higher priority, which can end the flight early once the goal is reached.

The setting that fits depends on what the deal is meant to do. A guaranteed deal that the buyer wants delivered steadily across a month usually belongs on 'Evenly', so the impressions arrive on the schedule the contract implies. A launch or a flight tied to an event can suit 'Frontloaded', where the audience is concentrated early. 'As fast as possible' fits a line item that should simply consume available inventory, for example a high-priority sponsorship that should take its share immediately. The wrong choice changes the curve in ways the deal did not ask for.

The cost of a mismatch shows up at the edges of the flight. A line item left on 'As fast as possible' or 'Frontloaded' when the deal wanted even delivery can exhaust its inventory in the first days, then sit idle while the buyer expected impressions through the end date. A line item that needed to move quickly but was left on 'Evenly' can hold back early and leave a scramble to catch up at the close, where the inventory to make up the gap may not exist. Google's reference on how line item delivery is paced sets out how each setting shapes the curve.

A few signs point to pacing that does not match the flight:
  • A guaranteed deal meant to run steadily is set to 'Frontloaded' or 'As fast as possible', so it spends inventory early and trails off
  • A line item that should move quickly is set to 'Evenly' and sits behind schedule for most of the flight
  • The delivery curve in the line item's report rises and falls in a shape the deal never intended
You may want to read each line item's setting against the deal it represents and the delivery curve it is actually producing, rather than assuming the default fits every case.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, directly sold guaranteed deals are often meant to run evenly across a sponsorship month, so a line item left on 'Frontloaded' can spend its inventory in the first week and leave the buyer short at the end. You may want to confirm each guaranteed line item's delivery setting matches the steady cadence the contract implies, then check the delivery curve in its report for a shape that holds across the flight.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, promotions tied to a sale window or a product launch can suit front-loaded pacing, where the audience and intent are concentrated at the start. You may want to confirm any line item set to 'Frontloaded' or 'As fast as possible' is one where early concentration is intended, rather than a steady deal that was left on the wrong setting.

learn_more.source_title: Set the delivery speed of line items
How line item delivery is paced

investigate.intro: Review each active line item's delivery setting in Delivery against what the deal is meant to do, and read the delivery curve in its report to confirm the pacing matches the intent.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Line items', and for a sample of active line items note the 'Deliver impressions' setting on each, which reads as 'Evenly', 'Frontloaded', or 'As fast as possible'.

For each sampled line item, note what the deal is meant to do, for example a guaranteed buy that should run steadily, a launch concentrated early, or a sponsorship that should take its share immediately.

Open the line item's report or delivery progress view and look at the delivery curve over the flight, noting whether impressions are spread evenly, weighted to the start, or trailing off before the end date.
Flag any line item whose setting and resulting curve do not match the deal, for example a steady guaranteed deal set to 'Frontloaded', or a fast-fill line item left on 'Evenly'.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per sampled line item, its delivery setting, what the deal is meant to do, and the shape of its delivery curve. A line item whose setting and curve match the intent passes, for example a steady guaranteed deal on 'Evenly' delivering on schedule. A line item whose setting works against the deal is a fail recorded with the line item name and the mismatch, for example a steady deal on 'Frontloaded' that spends its inventory early and trails off.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the delivery setting, the intended pacing, and the delivery curve for each sampled line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some line items on {{site_name}} carry a delivery setting that does not match how the deal is meant to pace across its flight. In Google Ad Manager, the 'Deliver impressions' setting decides whether a line item spreads its impressions evenly, front-loads them early, or takes them as fast as possible, so a mismatch spends inventory too early or holds it back too long. On {{site_name}}, this shows up as steady guaranteed deals set to front-loaded pacing that trail off before the end date, or fast-fill line items left on even delivery that sit behind schedule. {{voice_recommend}} setting each affected line item's delivery setting to match its intent, with steady deals on 'Evenly' and early-weighted flights on 'Frontloaded', then rechecking the delivery curve in each line item's report to confirm the pacing now holds across the flight.

audit_checks.title: Guaranteed line items are under-delivering
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A guaranteed or sponsorship line item that falls short of its goal forces make-goods or refunds, which costs revenue twice, once on the impressions never sold and once on the inventory given away to make up the gap. Because the line item still shows some delivery, a shortfall projected for the end of the flight is easy to miss until it is too late to recover.

educate.base:
A guaranteed line item in Google Ad Manager (GAM) carries a commitment to deliver a set number of impressions by the end date. When it falls short, the publisher owes a make-good, an extension or extra inventory given to the buyer to cover the gap, or a refund. Both outcomes cost revenue, and a repeated shortfall puts the relationship with the buyer at risk, so under-delivery is a yield problem and a trust problem at once.

The delivery indicator on each line item is the early warning. It compares the share of the goal delivered against the share of the flight elapsed, so a value below 100% means the line item is behind the pace it needs to finish on time. The indicator is updated roughly every 15 minutes, which gives the auditor a current read on whether a line item is on track or projected to fall short well before the end date arrives.

Under-delivery usually traces to one of a few causes. The forecast at booking may have been over-optimistic, so the goal was set higher than the inventory can support. The targeting may be narrow enough that the available impressions are fewer than the goal requires. A higher-priority line item competing for the same inventory may be taking the impressions this one needs because priority is evaluated before this line item gets its turn. For programmatic guaranteed deals, a low response rate from the buyer can also hold delivery back. Google's reference on how to troubleshoot Ad Manager line item delivery walks through the non-delivery reasons, and its reference on how to review a line item's delivery progress explains how to read the indicator.

A few signs point to guaranteed inventory that is projected to fall short:
  • A guaranteed line item's delivery indicator reads below 100%, so it is behind the pace it needs to finish on time
  • The projected delivery for the flight sits below the booked goal when a forecast is run on the line item
  • A higher-priority line item targets the same inventory and is taking the impressions this one needs
You may want to identify the cause of any shortfall while there is still flight remaining to recover, rather than discovering it only when the make-good is already owed.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, directly sold guaranteed deals are usually the revenue the operation is built on, so an under-delivering sponsorship is a make-good the publisher pays out of premium inventory. You may want to review the delivery indicator on each active guaranteed line item, and where one reads below 100%, run a forecast to see whether the goal, the targeting, or competition from a higher-priority line item is the cause while there is still time to adjust.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a guaranteed promotion for a sponsor or a brand partner carries the same make-good exposure as any sold deal. You may want to confirm any guaranteed line item tied to a dated campaign is projected to deliver in full before the campaign window closes. A shortfall discovered after the date has passed cannot be made up on that inventory.

learn_more.source_title: Troubleshoot Ad Manager line item delivery
Review a line item's delivery progress

investigate.intro: Review the delivery indicator and projected delivery for each active guaranteed line item, and for any that is behind, trace the shortfall to the goal, the targeting, or competition from higher-priority demand.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Line items', and filter to the guaranteed types, Sponsorship and Standard, that carry a delivery commitment.
For each active guaranteed line item, note the delivery indicator, which compares the share of the goal delivered against the share of the flight elapsed, and flag any reading below 100%.
Open the line item and run a forecast to see its projected delivery for the flight against the booked goal, noting whether the projection falls short.

For any line item projected to fall short, open its 'Troubleshoot' tab and review the non-delivery reasons, and check whether a higher-priority line item targets the same inventory and is taking the impressions this one needs.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active guaranteed line item, its delivery indicator, its projected delivery against the booked goal, and the cause of any shortfall. A guaranteed line item on track to meet its goal passes. A guaranteed line item whose indicator reads below 100% or whose projection falls short of the goal is a fail recorded with the line item name and the identified cause, for example an over-optimistic forecast, targeting too narrow for the available inventory, or competition from a higher-priority line item.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the delivery indicator, the projected delivery against goal, and the cause of any shortfall for each guaranteed line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some guaranteed line items on {{site_name}} are projected to fall short of their delivery goals. In Google Ad Manager, a guaranteed line item carries a commitment to deliver a set number of impressions, so a shortfall forces a make-good or a refund, which costs revenue on the impressions never sold and again on the inventory given away to cover the gap. On {{site_name}}, the delivery indicator on the affected line items reads below 100%, and the shortfall traces to causes such as an over-optimistic forecast at booking, targeting too narrow for the available inventory, or a higher-priority line item taking the same impressions. {{voice_recommend}} running a forecast on each affected line item while flight remains, then adjusting the goal, broadening the targeting, or resolving the competition for the inventory so the line item is projected to deliver in full before the end date.
